data.title: Bitcoin: The Brave Will Be Rewarded - Maintain A Strong Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Since my initiation, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has performed by +67% versus +10% for the S&P 500 (SPX). However, for about three months now, BTC has been ranging between $91,000 and $109,000. As I'm writing this article, it stands at $95,641.

In this logic, my last recommendation published on January 7, 2025, and which was my 3rd update on the matter, hasn't performed well.

Since I published it, BTC has been down -1.2% while the SPX has been up +3.48%. In the article that I invite you to look at, I mentioned a major catalyst that I expect to support BTC in 2025.

Despite these positive triggers I have identified, it is undeniable that the current range we are sitting in has brought a wave of pessimism among many investors. As a matter of fact, the BTC fear and greed index is now back in neutral territory.

In this article, I'm going to explain why I believe this situation represents a massive opportunity for investors to initiate or strengthen their BTC exposure.

U.S. States Adoption

Recently, we've seen many bills related to the establishment of a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve introduced in multiple U.S. states. As of today, 22 bills have been introduced in 22 different states. These states will soon have the responsibility to decide if they want to have the possibility to invest in BTC.

The process is long and can take anywhere from a few months to over a year.

After a bill is introduced in the state legislature, it gets debated in the first legislative committee. Then, it moves to the first chamber, where it is debated and voted on. If approved, the bill goes to a second committee in the other chamber for review. There, it is debated and voted on again. If both chambers pass the bill, it is sent to the governor for signing into law.

According to VanEck, if voted across all states, this could potentially represent 250 000 BTC purchased in the future. This is almost 1.15% of the total supply and 1.3% of the circulating supply.

In my opinion, these calculations must be considered carefully, as there is a high probability that not all states will cross the line and adopt BTC.

However, I still interpret this trend very positively. It proves BTC has come a long way and is now even being considered for an investment envelope that requires the highest level of scrutiny: state funds investing citizens money.

UAE - The First Country Checked Off my 2025 Bingo list... and More Will Join The Party

In my previous article, I shared my 2025 Bingo list, where I identified countries, I expected to embrace BTC this year. Among them, I notably mentioned Switzerland, Japan, the United Arab Emirates, and South American countries like Argentina.

A few days ago, an interesting news report came out.

According to Yahoo Finance: Abu Dhabi's sovereign wealth fund, Mubadala Investment Company, disclosed a $460 million stake in BlackRock's Bitcoin ETF, making it the seventh-largest holder of this ETF. In my opinion, this is just the beginning.

Now that the UAE has crossed the line, I expect another country to get in the game soon: Saudi Arabia. Why? Because these countries have historically competed, especially when it comes to investing. A few examples: both have aggressively deployed capital in the technology sector, both have fought to become the financial hub of the Middle East, and even in sports and entertainment, the UAE was the first to make significant investments in European football clubs, while Saudi Arabia followed by acquiring Newcastle United.

In my opinion, with Abu Dhabi's sovereign wealth fund now investing in Bitcoin ETFs, it's only a matter of time before Saudi Arabia follows.

Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund manages over $900 billion in assets, making it the 6th largest sovereign wealth fund globally. BTC aligns with the nation's goal of diversifying beyond oil. That's why I believe this country could be a potential buyer, and a game-changer for BTC.

What About the US Strategic Reserve?

First, an important element to consider: the U.S. still holds 207,189 BTC.

There was a non-zero probability that the Biden administration would sell BTC holdings before Trump took office, but it didn't happen. This is very positive, as it suggests a potential bipartisan consensus on the matter.

Since Trump has taken office, we haven't had any specific news about the so-called U.S. strategic reserve. However, another positive development has emerged. On February 3rd, President Trump signed an executive order for the creation of a Sovereign Wealth Fund. This comes along with the task assigned to Crypto Czar David Sacks to study the feasibility of a Bitcoin reserve or a digital asset stockpile.

In a sense, I believe these two elements are related and could both serve as positive catalysts for BTC. It seems plausible that the U.S. sovereign wealth fund, which is expected to total $5.7 trillion in assets, could also invest in Bitcoin, just like the UAE did. Trump has given 90 days to the Secretary of Commerce and the Secretary of the Treasury to create this fund, so we will have news soon enough.

In my opinion, these two developments represent real catalysts that will support BTC and strengthen its reputation as a reserve asset.

Speculation Rising over Fort Knox

Built in 1930, Fort Knox serves as the U.S. Bullion Depository, housing 147 million ounces of gold, worth approximately $425 billion. As gold prices rise and the Department of Government and Efficiency (DOGE) shakes up the U.S. administration, public interest in Fort Knox's actual reserves has surged.

Due to growing distrust over how poorly U.S. taxpayers' money has been managed over the past decade, many are questioning whether the reported gold reserves are accurate. This skepticism has only been reinforced by multiple sources alleging that the last official audit of Fort Knox took place back in 1974.

You might be wondering: how does this relate to BTC? If an audit is indeed conducted and reveals that gold reserves are missing, I have the conviction that it will reignite the gold vs. BTC debate. The idea of gold as the ultimate store of value would take a hit, and BTC, with its verifiable and transparent supply, could emerge as an even stronger alternative.

In a world where trust in traditional assets is eroding, BTC's appeal as a decentralized, non-manipulable form of wealth storage could gain significant traction.

To close this article, I wanted to share this data that, I believe, is crucial. It shows how the ETF and financialization of BTC represent a real advantage for BTC holders, driving demand and making BTC an even scarcer asset over the long term.

Indeed, as time goes by and halving makes it even harder to mine BTC every four years, the new BTC supply continues to decrease. Meanwhile, ETF demand appears strong even a year after their launch. As you can see, despite BTC's poor price action on a YTD basis, net inflows into U.S. spot BTC ETFs have outweighed new supply in 2025 by a factor of 2.68x.

Meanwhile, BTC reserves on exchanges continue to decrease and currently stand at 2.23 million.

Conclusion

Despite the current price action disappointing many BTC holders, I still believe there is significant upside potential in 2025, with major triggers and catalysts on the horizon. I consider the current level attractive, and I remain particularly optimistic about BTC's future.

Periods of consolidation often test investor patience, but history has shown that Bitcoin tends to move in strong cycles, with explosive price action following phases of accumulation. Given the current setup, I have the conviction that we are in such a phase, making this an attractive time to accumulate for those with a long-term view.

The braves will be rewarded. I remain optimistic about BTC's future and maintain my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: S&P 500 Records All-Time Highs Led By Chipmakers, Dow Jones Seesaw Continues
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The three major Wall Street indices fluctuated throughout the US session, shifting between gains and losses. A rise in chipmakers propelled the S&P 500 to fresh all-time highs late in the session despite a drop in technology stocks.

The S&P 500 edged up by 0.2%, while the Nasdaq 100 also posted a 0.2% gain. The Dow Jones Industrial Average showed mixed movements throughout the session. Meanwhile, the chipmakers index surged by 1.7%.

SPX 500 Top Movers

Intel’s (INTC) stock climbed on rumors about a possible company breakup. Super Micro Computer saw a big jump thanks to a positive outlook. Walgreens Boots Alliance rose after CNBC reported that Sycamore Partners might still be planning a takeover. Meanwhile, Meta’s 20-day streak of gains came to an end.

More Tariffs Ahead?

Yesterday we heard more from President Trump on proposed tariffs that may be announced. President Trump said we should see some announcements in the coming week, while once again lamenting that the EU has been an unfair trading partner.

The President touched on specific sectors, with Pharmaceuticals and Automobiles both expected to get hit with tariffs of around 25%. Tuesday’s comments are his most detailed yet in specifying other sectors that would be hit with fresh barriers.

It will be interesting to see what effect such announcements may have on stock prices moving forward following what has been quite an impressive earning season for US companies.

FOMC Minute and PMI Data Ahead

It is fair to say that following last week’s uptick in US inflation and drop off in retail sales numbers, market participants are once more focused on the Fed’s next moves. The FOMC minutes will be released later today, these should provide further insights into how the Fed sees tariffs impacting their monetary policy course and objectives moving forward.

The week will wrap up with S&P PMI data, which will give us another glimpse into the performance of the US economy from both a manufacturing and service perspective.

Technical Analysis

S&P 500

From a technical standpoint, the S&P has printed a fresh all-time high and could be due a pullback soon.

There was a triangle breakout which occurred as far back as September 19, with a target price of around 6170, which is now just 40 points away.

If we reach this level, I do think we could be in for a short-term correction, however, I expect such a move to be met with buying pressure if the current status quo remains the same.

It is hard to find key levels as we continue to trade at fresh highs with no historical price action to serve as a guide.

However, immediate focus for me will be 6170 and 6200 on the upside.

If price pushes lower from here, support may be found at 6100, 6080 (20-day MA).

S&P 500 Daily Chart, February 19, 2025

Support

  • 6100
  • 6080
  • 6050

Resistance

  • 6170
  • 6200
  • 6250

Original Post

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Nike's First-Ever Collab Lifts Shares
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nike (NKE) rallies after picking Kim Kardashian as its first-ever partner for a new brand. (00:23) Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) to pay €326M to settle tax case in Italy - report. (01:17) La-Z-Boy (LZB) sets cautious outlook based on soft housing market. (01:46)

This is an abridged transcript.

Nike (NYSE:NKE) was the biggest gainer in the Dow Jones Industrial Average on Tuesday after announcing that a new brand, called NikeSKIMS, will launch this spring in the U.S.

The collaboration with Kim Kardashian's SKIMS will include training apparel, footwear, and accessories that Nike says combine the company’s sport science with Skims' "obsession" for the female form.

Notably, the development marks the first time Nike (NYSE:NKE) has partnered with an outside company to launch a new brand.

Analysts have weighed in favorably on the upside for Nike (NKE), especially for the long term, on the view that it can add market share in a growing category.

Jefferies analyst Randal Konik said the firm views the NikeSKIMS partnership favorably because it is a promising step forward in the fitness and activewear market.

Shares of Nike (NKE) gained 6.2% on Tuesday.

Milan's prosecutors said they requested to drop a case against the European unit of Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google after the company agreed to pay €326M (about $340M) to settle a tax claim.

Reuters reported that the settlement spans the period between 2015 and 2019 and covers sanctions, penalties and interest, the report added, citing a statement from prosecutors.

Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

La-Z-Boy (NYSE:LZB) reported better-than-expected results for the fiscal third quarter, but amid expectations for “continued challenging macro conditions,” the furniture company set conservative guidance that was below Wall Street’s expectations.

Buoyed by a 4% improvement in sales and widening operating margin, La-Z-Boy (NYSE:LZB) realized a 1% increase in profits on a per share basis and a penny above Wall Street’s expectations. Total revenue of $522M was also better than anticipated, driven by a 7% increase in same-store sales.

In the wholesale segment, sales were up 2% to $363M due to gains in the North America market that were partially offset by the impact of what was labeled "significant customer transition," most likely a result of the stronger dollar, or increased prices.

Citing both the lingering challenges in the housing market and broader macro conditions, La-Z-Boy (NYSE:LZB) expects Q4 sales to be in the range of $545M to $565M, compared to $553.5M in the same quarter last year and below the consensus estimate of $566.2M. Operating margin is targeted for 8.5% to 9.5% versus 9.1% last year. These estimates do not assume any negative impact from impending U.S. trade policy.

Finally, the company said it will pay an unchanged quarterly dividend of $0.22 per share on March 14 to shareholders of record as of March 4.

Moomoo is an advanced investing trading platform that integrates real-time and comprehensive data with no commission on options trading, stocks or ETFs. New users from Seeking Alpha can exclusively enjoy an 8.1% APY* account opening bonus, up to 15 free stocks, and up to $300 in cash rewards. Terms & Conditions apply, visit moomoo x seeking alpha special offer for more details

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

RFK Jr. tells HHS staffers childhood vaccines, psychiatric drugs on his radar

Elon Musk, DOGE score victory against state AGs seeking to block access

Trump says 25% tariffs targeting cars, chips & pharma imports coming as soon as April 2

Catalyst watch:

  • The CAGNY Conference will include presentations from PepsiCo (PEP), Altria (MO), Molson Coors (TAP), and Philip Morris International (PM).

  • Notable investor events include BrightView Holdings' (BV) Investor Day and Lam Research's (LRCX) Investor Day.

  • Anbio Biotechnology (NNNN) is expected to start trading after the company prices its IPO.

  • Apple (AAPL) will announce the launch of a new product at a launch event. The announcement is expected to be conducted through a press release and promotional materials rather than a live-streamed keynote. A fourth-generation iPhone SE is widely expected to be featured at the event, although there is speculation about a new MacBook Air, new iPad Air, or a surprise reveal.

  • JetBlue Airways Corporation (JBLU) will participate in a fireside chat at the Barclays 42nd Annual Industrial Select Conference.

  • United Airlines (UAL) will present at the Barclays Industrial Select Conference.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 0.7% at nearly $72/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.5% at $96,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.2% and the DAX is down 0.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Bumble (NASDAQ:BMBL) shares plunged 18% after Q4 results revealed a decline in Total Average Revenue per Paying User (ARPPU) to $20.58 from $22.64 a year ago, with Q1 2025 revenue projected to decline 7%-10% Y/Y to $242M-$248M, falling short of the $255.48M consensus.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Housing Starts and Permits

  • 10:00 am E-Commerce Retail Sales

  • 2:00 pm FOMC Minutes

  • 5:00 pm Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson speaks on "Household Balance Sheets" before the Martin H. Crego Lecture in Economics event hosted by Vassar College.

data.title: Can Earnings Growth Offset Trade Headwinds For Canada?
data.description:
While there is no shortage of risks facing the Canadian market, the TSX is not far from all-time highs. MoneyTalk’s Greg Bonnell discusses how fundamentals for Canadian stocks are looking with Jennifer Nowski, Managing Director and Portfolio Manager at TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: While there are no shortage of risks globally and here at home, the TSX Composite Index is not far from all-time highs. Joining us now to discuss how to look for where things may go from here is Jennifer Nowski, Managing Director and Portfolio Manager with TD Asset Management. Great to have you back on the program as always, Jennifer.

And yeah, we constantly talk about the risks. We see them in the headlines. But we take a look at the TSX Composite Index. We're not that far from the best we've ever had, in terms of where we're at. I'm rambling. Let's get to you. Where are the Canadian markets right now?

Jennifer Nowski: Yeah. So thanks for having me on. So actually, coming into this year, the TSX had a couple tailwinds behind it in the form of better expected earnings growth and the positive impact of the Bank of Canada rate cuts. Now, the big risk, outstanding, is the potential for US tariff changes.

Now, as you alluded to, the TSX has been doing pretty well. In 2024, it was up nearly 22% And that was led by the technology, financials, and commodity sector. Turning to 2025, the outlook for earnings on the TSX is actually positive. Earnings are expected to grow about 11%, year over year, this year. And that creates a positive backdrop for the market because over the long term, if earnings are growing, it generally leads the stock market higher.

Now, as we dig into the earnings growth and where it's expected to come from, it is actually fairly broad-based. It does vary a bit between sectors, of course. And one thing you have to consider with TSX earnings is they are, of course, influenced by commodities. And actually, though, if, say, spot gold prices were to stay at their elevated levels, that would be a positive contributor for the gold sector. They would generate some good growth this year.

From a more macro perspective, as we know, the Bank of Canada has been cutting rates, and that will serve to take some of the pressure off consumers and businesses and could lead to better economic activity later in the year. Now, in terms of valuation, yes, the multiple for the TSX has expanded over the past year. The forward multiple, or PE, is now sitting at around 15 and 1/2 times. That's about one turn or so above its long-run average. So it's still within range and not within-- at an extreme level.

Now, the big uncertainty facing the market right now is potential changes to US tariff policy. Now, at this point, it's a very fluid situation. However, the ultimate outcome and impact of that is unknown.

Greg Bonnell: All right. So we have this unknown that's hanging over us. That's a binary-- one of your colleagues earlier in the week said, binary situation. Either it happens, or it doesn't happen. When we talk about that overhang of the tariff threat, how does that play out?

Jennifer Nowski: So I feel, in general, whenever I talk about tariffs, that anything I say goes out of date fairly quickly. But at a high level, the whole tariff negotiation creates two challenges for the market because it creates uncertainty and then, potentially, lower GDP growth later on, depending on what happens.

Now, taking the uncertainty piece of it, the market generally doesn't like uncertainty. It's hard to gauge what could potentially happen. But from a more real economic perspective, it also hinders businesses and consumers from making big decisions. So it could slow near-term growth. The other thing is it can distort, say, near-term earnings or economic data as people and businesses try to perhaps adjust their behaviors in anticipation, perhaps, of tariffs potentially coming.

Now, the ultimate outcome is unknown. But if tariffs were implemented, the sizing, timing, extent-- that will determine the impact. It would be a negative for GDP growth in Canada and a headwind for the US, too, because replacing-- or finding cost-effective substitutes for some imports isn't easy. It could be more costly.

The other thing to bear in mind, though, is there are potential offsets to tariffs. So from a Canadian perspective, that might come in the form of a weaker Canadian dollar or additional fiscal and monetary policy supports. So certainly, the tariff negotiations are a risk worth watching.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about that weaker Canadian dollar, because obviously, we have seen our currency pull back amid everything that's going on. How does loonie weakness play into the Canadian investing landscape?

Jennifer Nowski: Yeah, so from a TSX or investing perspective, the weaker Canadian dollar basically supports exporters or companies that earn revenues in US dollars but have a cost basis in Canadian dollars. So if you look at the commodity players, commodity prices are priced in US dollars, so they earn US dollar revenue. And many of them that are listed on the TSX have their operations in Canada. So they benefit from the weaker Canadian dollar supporting the revenue side, all else being equal.

The other impact is more of an accounting translation effect, so for businesses that have US operations and translate that income back into Canadian dollars. So that's in areas like technology, some financials, and industrials.

Now, the businesses that have more of a challenge are those that are importing goods from the US. So for example, grocers might face higher costs, and they'll have to manage how they purchase and how they pass that through the consumer. Now, one thing to bear in mind is that often, companies have hedging programs. So in the near term, some of this doesn't maybe flow through quite as expected or as quickly.

Greg Bonnell: When people think about the Canadian market-- and that's what we're talking about here-- they think about dividend players. Let's talk about the performance of some of the higher dividend-yielding stocks. What are you seeing out there?

Yeah, so 2024 was fairly good for the higher dividend-yielding sectors, with the financials and pipelines actually outperforming the TSX. Utilities and REITs also generated positive returns. But the real laggard was telecom. Now, if we take a look at how dividend yielders performed throughout the year, things generally really picked up in the summer. And that's after we had the initial Bank of Canada rate cuts. And then it became-- it was becoming apparent that the Fed was going to cut rates, too.

So the stocks rallied through the fall, and then, I'd say, leveled out near year-end. And that's because the 10-year yield, which fell, initially, with the rate cuts, rebounded and has been, perhaps, stickier. So when we think about the valuation for the dividend yielders, looking at the direction of that 10-year yield will be a key influence through 2025.

Now, I'll dive into two sectors, the laggard and one of the leaders. So starting with the laggard, and that was telecom, which was down during the year. And really, it was more of an industry issue, with heightened competition pressuring prices, that made revenue growth negligible. Looking forward, there's some concerns that with slower population growth in Canada this year, might be more challenging environment.

While there might be some early signs that pricing is stabilizing, it's still something that investors are watching carefully, and we need to see that sustained for longer. The other concern is more stock-specific within the sector. Some of the players do have to lower their leverage, and investors want to see more progress on that. One thing they could do, potentially, is sell assets, for example.

One area that did much better, though, was the pipelines and midstream sector. And that was a benefit of rates, but also a better macro environment in that industry. Oil prices were range-bound, but that's generally-- that's been a healthy environment for their producer customers. Many of them have hit or near their leverage targets and are in a good position to fund some modest growth.

And there is modest growth opportunities for them in, particularly, connecting LNG facilities with gas supply, as well as providing gas supply for growing power demand, as well as some of the renewables growth that these companies have.

Original Post

data.title: AI A Key Driver Of The Power Outlook
data.description:
Transcript

1) A driver of volatility

Markets recognizing the AI buildout’s huge power needs triggered a surge in AI-linked stocks last year, including utility stocks.

DeepSeek briefly challenged that trade in January as markets feared heavy investment in AI and estimates of its power needs may be overdone.

2) The demand for power

AI is set to be a key driver of new power demand as the mega-cap tech companies funding the AI buildout stick with or even increase their spending.

Other drivers of power demand include rising global incomes, reshoring, industrial growth and building cooling needs.

3) An evolving energy transition

Spiking power demand could add to the already huge investment expected in the energy transition and influence how the transition evolves, in our view.

In places like the U.S. where AI is a key driver of new power demand, we could see increased emphasis on natural gas and renewables.

We see investment opportunities across power generation, grid infrastructure and the electrical equipment value chain. We like utilities and power producers.

We see structural shifts like AI transforming economies and driving energy demand. Case in point: 2024’s surge in typically dull utility stocks and other AI-related companies. News of a seemingly more efficient AI model by China’s DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) briefly interrupted this rally. We don’t see DeepSeek’s innovations hurting power demand, nor do the large AI builders. AI is one of many factors driving greater energy demand. We eye beneficiaries in utilities and infrastructure.

Buzz about utilities

Returns for selected AI-exposed utilities versus the S&P 500, 2024-2025

We see a transformation underway potentially requiring investment on par with the industrial revolution. This can drive market volatility, as we have seen. One example: markets keying on AI’s power needs triggered a surge in AI-linked stocks last year, pushing the usually staid utilities sector higher. DeepSeek briefly challenged that trade last month on fears that heavy AI capex, and estimates of AI’s power needs, may be overdone. See the chart. Yet, the selloff quickly reversed, a sign markets now share our view that while DeepSeek could deliver efficiency gains, new frontiers – like artificial general intelligence that matches or surpasses human abilities – will likely still require large investment. Plus, AI efficiency gains could accelerate the AI mega force, creating the need for more energy and the infrastructure to support it. That’s backed up by mega cap tech’s plans to stick with and even increase AI capex.

In the U.S., where AI is shaping up to be a key driver of new power demand, estimates of the scale of the nation’s total power needs are huge and growing. Q4 corporate earnings season shows the mega cap tech companies driving the AI buildout are largely sticking with their AI capex, even after the DeepSeek news. Some are even increasing on last year’s spend – Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOGL) are expected to invest nearly $320 billion in AI capex this year, up roughly 40% from 2024, according to company guidance. The AI buildout could drive power demands further afield, too: many governments are joining U.S. companies in their AI commitments – as seen in the $500 billion U.S. Stargate project and the European Union’s €200 billion AI bet announced in Paris last week.

The broader surge in power demand

Yet, AI is only part of a broader trend of growing power needs globally. Other drivers include rising global incomes, reshoring, industrial growth and building cooling needs. Extreme heat is driving air conditioning use, particularly in emerging markets where it is not yet widespread. Electrification of buildings and vehicles is expanding, though unevenly across regions. We expect speedbumps as soaring demand runs up against power supply constraints, potentially adding to inflation pressures. This calls for upping allocations to equities, infrastructure and inflation-linked bonds as an inflation hedge, we think.

Assessing what type of power will meet this spiking power demand is key, we think. Already huge investment expected in the energy transition could grow further, in our view. The companies driving the AI buildout prefer power supplies that are always on, cost-effective, immediately available and low carbon, a difficult combination. That could drive U.S. demand for natural gas and renewables. Yet, we still see a role for traditional energy as policymakers seek affordable and reliable power alongside low-carbon goals. Longer term, demand could rise for nuclear and other low-carbon, available-when-needed energy sources. We eye investment opportunities across power generation, grid infrastructure and the electrical equipment value chain. We like utilities and power producers in this environment and get granular to identify opportunities along the supply chain.

Our bottom line

We see soaring power demand driving bouts of market volatility. We get active to find investment opportunities – like in utilities, grids and electrical equipment supply chains – and value selectivity as performance dispersion grows.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: Intact Financial: Keep Your Cash Intact With A 6% Preferred Yield
data.description:
Intact Financial Corporation (TSX:IFC:CA) is the dominant Property & Casualty Insurer in Canada. After reaching market dominance in Canada by acquisition, the company has expanded into the U.S. and U.K. by further acquisitions in recent years.

P&C Insurance has gone through a difficult period, dealing with the impacts of climate change, the covid crisis, and subsequent inflation spike, and through all of this IFC has grown revenues and profits considerably. The market has taken note of this, and this has driven the share price up significantly, doubling the returns of the TSX benchmark in the last 5 years.

IFC has seemed to be reaching extreme over-valuation levels. The chart below compares IFC to a range of peers in the U.S. and Canada. The valuation gap is huge of price to earnings.

And this might be explained, if IFC was a massive growth stock. Actually, compared to the peer group, IFC revenues have underperformed most of the P&C insurance peers.

Perhaps IFC has been insanely profitable? Nope. Again, on this metric, IFC performance has been at the lower end of the pack.

So I remain baffled by the IFC valuation. I was a happy investor until I felt that the IFC valuation had got ahead of its ski’s.

I like the company, and think that it has a great position in the Canadian market, while being less confident of its overseas acquisitions. However, I can find no reason for the valuation to keep soaring above peers.

As such I reluctantly decided to step to the sidelines on the common shares a while back. To be fair, I made the call early, and have left potential capital gains on the table, however, I exited with a tidy profit.

Now IFC is a great company, as I have said. I like being a shareholder, but not at anY price. I have to assume that at some stage their valuation will come back down to earth.

IFC has a solid balance sheet, and enjoys an A+ credit rating from AM Best. Their policyholder, and their lenders can feel secure, even if their combined share investors look to be vulnerable to multiple compression.

One option for investors is to consider taking a fixed income position. I decided to sell my own shares (early but profitably) and relocate to some of their preferred shares.

I’ll look into one of my picks below.

IFC.PR.G:CA

The G series preferred shares is one of my picks.

Preferred shares in Canada commonly pay a fixed dividend at a rate which re-sets every five years at a fixed percentage above the Government of Canada five year interest rate. Like bonds, the shares can be called for par value on the launch date every five years.

Intact's G series follows this format, and resets every five years at a GOC rate plus 2.55%. The G series last reset in June 2023, and will hold the current rate until the next reset date in June 2028. The shares have a par value of $25, and currently trade close to par at $24.62. Buyers today would receive a yield of 6.1%.

The current GOC 5 year rate is 2.78%. If rates are unchanged by the next reset date on June 30th 2028, the yield would drop to 5.43%

Trading close to par, the G series reflects a neutral view on rates in 3.5 years time.

Investors who are happy with a 6.1% yield for 3.5 years, and a 2.55% spread over GOV 5 year rates thereafter should find IFC G attractive.

Redemption - IFC do have the option to redeem shares in June 2025. IFC has fixed rate preferred series with yields between 5.2% and 5.4%, and another floater, IFC.C, which has a higher spread, and resets in 2026.

I’d say that redemption is relatively unlikely as IFC have found this level of funding cost to be efficient through the rate cycle, however, there is a risk of redemption in lower rate scenarios.

Stable Rates

In a stable rate environment, the yield will drop to 5.43%.

Rising Rates

If inflation takes hold again, and rates rise, investors will capitalise on this in 2028, earnings 6.1% in the interim. Series G owners will only lose out if rates rise above the initial June 2023 reset rate level.

Falling Rates

As mentioned above in a very low rate scenario the G yield would drop, and likely generate mark to market losses. Investors who are sensitive to absolute yield, rather than spread might prefer fixed rate alternatives.

Conclusion

  • IFC is a great company, but common shares are currently over valued in my opinion.
  • Preferred shares offer a way to invest in IFC at a decent valuation and earn competitive yields.
  • The G series offers a secure rate until June 2028, and a decent spread over the GOC 5 year after that.
  • I hold series G, and plan to do so until 6 months to reset, then re-evaluate.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BNY Mellon Core Plus Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
“We expect 2024 to give way to a softer, though still positive year of growth in 2025, of ~2.2%. We expect inflation to trend lower, but progress is likely to remain slow.”

Market Review

President-elect Donald Trump prevailed in the US Presidential election. He won the popular vote, while the Republican Party secured a majority in the Senate and a narrow majority in the House of Representatives.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut the Federal Reserve (the “Fed”) funds rate by 25 basis points(bp) in November and again in December, taking it to a range of 4.25% to 4.5%. Chair Powell noted that electing to cut in December was a “closer call”. The central bank adjusted its median “dot plot” interest rate projections to reflect two rate cuts in 2025, down from the four it projected in September. The FOMC increased both its headline and core inflation projections for end-2025 from 2.1% and 2.2% respectively to 2.5%.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended December 31, 2024, the Fund’s Class I shares returned -3.27%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, returned -3.06% for the same period.

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Performance for periods less than 1 year is not annualized. Go to BNY Mellon Investment Management - Global Home - English for the fund's most recent month-end returns. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Total Expenses (12/31/24)

1 Gross expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, before any fee waivers or expense reimbursements. 2 Net Expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, after any applicable fee waivers or expense reimbursements. The net Expense ratio(s) reflect a contractual expense reduction agreement through 8/30/2025, without which, the returns would have been lower. The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Employment data was volatile, distorted by hurricanes and strikes, but continued to display a modest easing of labor market conditions. The unemployment rate started and ended the period at 4.2%. Inflation edged up, with Consumer Price Index (CPI) rising from 2.5% to 2.7% and Personal Consumer Expenditures (PCE) from 2.3% to 2.4%. Core CPI rose from 3.2% to 3.3% and Core PCE from 2.7% to 2.8%. Nonetheless, the crucial “stickier” core services categories, like shelter, transportation and medical services showed some encouraging improvement. Q3 Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth was revised up from 2.8% to 3.1%, with revisions to consumption and net exports the largest drivers.

Following the election, the market initially reacted with a “risk-on move”, with high-beta US equities outperforming and credit spreads rallying, albeit markets retraced some of the rally in December. Political uncertainty relating to potential policies, such as tariffs, helped drive some volatility during the quarter.

Treasuries sold off, underperforming other developed bond markets, with yields rising by up to 83bp. This move fully disinverted the US yield curve. US investment grade credit spreads narrowed by 8bp (albeit they underperformed euro, UK and Australian investment grade markets) for an excess return of 82bp. US high yield markets narrowed by 12bp, for an excess return of 1.2%.

Performance Review

The fund entered the quarter with a modestly long duration and curve steepening bias relative to the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Both detracted as interest rates rose and the US Treasury curve flattened. In plus sectors, relative value trades in inflation and non-US rates also detracted.

Security selection in corporates contributed positively, notably selection in energy and banks. Selection in mortgage-backed securities (MBS) was a slight detractor from relative performance. Overall credit and sector allocation was positive with overweight allocations to high yield and Investment grade credit contributing. From a more granular sector perspective, the overweight allocations to energy and asset-backed securities (ABS) were additive to performance.

The fund entered Q4 having reduced credit risk significantly with the bulk of the reduction having come in midstream energy and junior subordinated banks. After the US presidential election, credit risk was increased in line with an improved outlook for economic growth. Longer duration investment grade credit was slightly decreased in favor of shorter duration high yield creating a net gain in overall credit exposure. At the industry level, the fund focused on additions in utilities, technology, media, and telecommunications (TMT), and food & beverages. Additionally, the fund increased exposure to securitized assets given attractive valuations offered by agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), esoteric ABS, and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs). With respect to interest rate positioning, the fund began the quarter positioned for a steeper yield curve and lower interest rates given an outlook for more rate cuts and large, long-term fiscal deficits. The team removed the long duration position early in the quarter and modestly reduced the positioning for a steeper yield curve though we remain focused on the economic implications for long-term rates given the current fiscal environment.

Market Outlook

We expect 2024 to give way to a softer, though still positive year of growth in 2025, of ~2.2%. We expect inflation to trend lower, but progress is likely to remain slow. We believe the incoming administration’s economic agenda creates some uncertainty. Looser fiscal policy is likely to provide a positive impetus to growth, while a potential mass deportation program may do the opposite. Tariffs may have the potential to raise inflation. We expect the Fed is likely to continue its easing cycle, but likely with a slower and more considered approach toward the neutral rate. Rising government bond yields mean “all-in” corporate bonds yields remain attractive relative to history, in our view.

We believe underlying corporate fundamentals remain strong while policy easing and a resilient labor market bode well for credit. Nonetheless, other markets, like European credit can offer relative value, reflecting the softer economic environment.

Global high yield markets appear to be characterized by strong demand. Issuance has been driven primarily by companies refinancing debt due to limited leveraged buy-out activity, leaving investors facing challenges to reinvest proceeds from maturing bonds and higher coupon payments. Given high absolute yields, we expect the favorable technical environment to continue. We expect default rates to remain subdued thanks to resilient growth, better-than-expected earnings, and improved capital market access.

We have adopted a more cautious active stance in the near term. We anticipate significant investment grade supply in H1 2025 as issuers may seek to capitalize on strong demand. This could exert some upward pressure on spreads, creating potential opportunities. Careful security selection and credit research remains critical to ensure that business models remain robust despite higher funding costs.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, or a summary prospectus, if available, that contains this and other information about a fund, contact your financial professional or visit BNY Mellon Investment Management - Global Home - English. Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

* The BNY Mellon Core Plus Fund ("Fund") commenced operations after the assets of a predecessor mutual fund reorganized into the fund on 2/2/18. Performance for Class Y is the performance from the predecessor fund. The predecessor fund was the Cutwater Investment Grade Bond Fund, Institutional Class, incepted on 12/2/2010, and was renamed to the Insight Investment Grade Bond Fund following BNY’s purchase of Cutwater Asset Management on 1/2/2015. The total return performance figures for Class A and I shares of the Fund represent the performance of the Funds Class Y shares for periods prior to 2/2/18, the inception date for Class A and I shares, and the performance of Class A and I shares, from that inception date. Performance reflects the applicable class distribution/servicing fees since the inception date. Investors should consider, when deciding whether to purchase a particular class of shares, the investment amount, class restrictions, anticipated holding period and other relevant factors. Additionally, on 10/19/2018 the Fund received the merged assets of Dreyfus Intermediate Term Income Fund, the performance of which is not reflected above.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio’s other investments. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging-market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer’s ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. Mortgage-backed securities: Ginnie Maes and other securities backed by the full faith and credit of the United States government are guaranteed only as to the timely payment of interest and principal when held to maturity. The market prices for such securities are not guaranteed and will fluctuate. Privately issued mortgage-related securities also are subject to credit risks associated with the underlying mortgage properties. These securities may be more volatile and less liquid than more traditional, government-backed debt securities.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS and CMBS (agency and nonagency. The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes USD-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility and financial issuers. The Bloomberg High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody’s, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. Bonds from issuers with an emerging markets country of risk, based on Bloomberg’s EM country definition, are excluded. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS) is an investment similar to a bond that is made up of a bundle of home loans bought from the banks that issued them. Investors in MBS receive periodic payments similar to bond coupon payments. Asset-Backed Security (ABS) is a financial security such as a bond or note which is collateralized by a pool of assets such as loans, leases, credit card debt, royalties, or receivables. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, such as transportation, food, and medical care. A yield curve is a line that plots yields, or interest rates, of bonds that have equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country’s borders in a specific time period. Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) also known as consumer spending, is a measure of the spending on goods and services by people of the United States. A yield curve is a line that plots the yields or interest rates of bonds that have equal credit quality but different maturity dates. Investment grade refers to the quality of a company's credit. To be considered an investment grade issue, the company must be rated at 'BBB' or higher by Standard and Poor's or 'Baa" or higher by Moody's. Sticky is a general economics term that can apply to any financial variable that is resistant to change. In finance, esoteric refers to investments that are complex, unconventional, and not well understood. These investments are often less liquid and can be riskier than more traditional investments.

This material has been provided for informational purposes only and should not be construed as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular investment product, strategy, investment manager or account arrangement, and should not serve as a primary basis for investment decisions. Prospective investors should consult a legal, tax or financial professional in order to determine whether any investment product, strategy or service is appropriate for their particular circumstances. Views expressed are those of the author stated and do not reflect views of other managers or the firm overall. Views are current as of the date of this publication and subject to change. This information contains projections or other forward-looking statements regarding future events, targets or expectations, and is only current as of the date indicated. There is no assurance that such events or expectations will be achieved, and actual results may be significantly different from that shown here. The information is based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate and may be superseded by subsequent market events or for other reasons. References to specific securities, asset classes and financial markets are for illustrative purposes only and are not intended to be and should not be interpreted as recommendations.

Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission.

data.title: Herc Holdings: Weak Local Account Demand Expected To Temper Short-Term Growth
data.description:
The Thesis

As this equipment rental supplier, Herc Holdings (NYSE:HRI), exited the year, it delivered double-digit growth across its topline due to robust performance, particularly in its national accounts business, which benefited from healthy megaproject activity throughout 2024. However, the local account growth remains subdued due to the continued softer demand environment. I expect the company's overall topline growth to remain under pressure in FY25 as the elevated interest rate environment across the region continues to impact the local project activity negatively, impacting the demand for the company's rental services. In my view, this continued volume pressure across most of the company's business should also impact the margin growth in the quarters ahead, despite the recent single-digit price increase. Since my last bullish rated article on HRI, the stock is up decently, around 30%. While I remain optimistic about the company's long-term growth prospect due to infrastructure spending tailwinds and its focus on acquisitions and efficiency improvements, its near-term outlook appears weak, which along with the stocks' valuation near historical levels, I am downgrading HRI to a HOLD for now.

Last Quarter Highlights

After delivering single digit growth throughout the year, the company's consolidated topline was up in high teens as it exited 2024, primarily due to rental revenue growth driven by mega projects, contribution from recent acquisitions and easier year-on-year comparison with single digit growth in the prior year's quarter. The company's topline grew approximately 14.4% year-on-year reaching $951 million, with an organic growth of about 6.5% in the fourth quarter of 2024. For the full year 2024, the company's consolidated topline grew 8.7% versus FY23 to $3.6 billion, primarily due to moderate growth in the initial quarter of the 2024.

During the quarter, while the volume remains healthy, the benefit from favorable pricing and cost discipline helped the company, offsetting the impact from reduced fleet efficiency in slower local market demand. As a result, HRI reported a nearly flat growth in its adjusted EBITDA margins to 46.1% during the quarter from 46% a year ago. EBITDA growth during the quarter also benefited the company's bottom-line growth as its adjusted EPS increased to $3.58 from $3.24 a year ago, however, missed the consensus estimates by $0.36 in the fourth quarter of FY24.

Exiting the year, HRI saw double-digit growth in the last quarter after experiencing moderate single digit growth throughout 2024. While the company continued to outpace the overall rental market primarily due to its robust national accounts business led by mega projects, the local market demand environment remains soft due to elevated interest rate environment across the region. Considering a significant share of the local account business of nearly 54% in the company's total revenue, I expect the continued headwind for this segment due to high interest-rate environment should put pressure on the overall topline growth in the quarters ahead as it continues to influence customers spending decisions leading to reduced new projects starts.

However, the national account business is expected to remain healthy due to strong funding and opportunities from infrastructure projects on more mega projects related to LNG, data centers, semiconductors, healthcare and education which are expected to ramp up in 2025. In my view, this along with projects initiated in the latter half of 2024 along with benefit from the recent rental rate increase should support the topline growth in 2025, partially offsetting the impact from weak local market demand. As a result, despite robust activity across the national account business, I expect the company to deliver its topline growth in mid to low to mid-single digits in the quarters ahead, in line with the company's guidance of 4%-6% of rental revenue growth for FY25.

Although, the pressure from soft demand in the local business is expected to continue in the short term, the long term remains favorable as project pipeline remain robust. With nearly $357 billion in infrastructure projects being forecasted for 2025 and many more in the next 3 years, HRI appears to be only at the beginning of a strong activity period in the industry in the coming years. HRI is targeting to capture 10% to 15% of these opportunities driven by significant investments, which should support the company's national account business in FY25 and beyond. Apart from this, the company has also added nearly 51 businesses and 81 greenfield locations through acquisition in the past few years, which are anticipated to contribute meaningfully to overall revenue growth as they mature in the next 2-3 years.

Overall, the company's long-term prospects remain promising due to tailwinds from significant infrastructure spending on mega projects in the coming years, particularly related to LNG, data centers, and semiconductors, which, along with HRI's diversified market positions across various end markets and its continued focus on market share expansion through strategic M&As and investment in new equipment, should drive topline growth in the coming years. However, talking about the short-term demand environment, the company continues to face pressure from subdued growth in its local accounts business due to lower project activity and discouraged customer spending by elevated interest rates, which are expected to largely offset the continued growth in the national account business, impacting the overall topline growth in FY25.

Valuation and Takeaway

I last covered HRI in September last year with a bullish view due to decent short-term growth prospects and an attractive valuation multiple. Since that article, the stock has grown nearly 30%, significantly outperforming its peers stock performance. Currently, the company's stock is trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 15.21, based on its FY25 EPS estimates of $13.13, representing a low single digit increase from a year ago. In comparison to its historical average multiple of 16.37x, the stock appears to be trading at a slight discount. While the stock looks a bit cheaper than its five-year average P/E, its forward growth has also moderated significantly to just mid-single digit as compared to the strong double-digit growth it has experienced in the past years, as can also be seen in the company growth table below.

While the company appears to be strongly positioned to benefit from a robust mega project pipeline driven by significant infrastructure spending in the coming years, the near term demand environment remains soft. In my view, the company's local account business should remain under pressure going forward as the elevated interest rate environment continues to affect the project starts due to discouraged customer spending decisions across the region.

I believe this should result in continued volume pressure in the local accounts business, which accounts for over 54% of total revenue share, further impacting the margins and bottom line in the short term. The company's interest burden has also increased slightly due to increased debt to fund the acquisition completed in 2024, which should also impact the company's bottom-line performance, further deteriorating the stock valuation in the quarters ahead. Therefore, despite a favorable long-term outlook, uncertainty in the near term due to a softer demand environment across most of the business and a valuation multiple near the historical average suggests staying on the sidelines for now. Hence, I am downgrading the stock to a "HOLD" rating for now.

Source: FactSet. As of 12/31/2024. Past performance is not a reliable indicator or guarantee of future results. Due to market volatility, the market may not perform in a similar manner in the future. Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and are not available for direct investment. The index data provided is not representative of any Lord Abbett product.

Market Review

  • In the fourth quarter, U.S. Treasury yields rose significantly, and the yield curve steepened, resulting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index ending the quarter down over 3.0%, while U.S. equities experienced positive returns, led by a strong rally in large cap growth stocks. Markets were driven by the November presidential election, which saw a surge in investor optimism due to expectations of stronger economic growth, improved profit forecasts, and a more favorable regulatory environment. There were several bearish factors, however, including uncertainties regarding the incoming administration's policy proposals, debt and deficit concerns, and strong dollar headwinds. Economic data was mixed, with a rebound in November nonfarm payrolls but stalled progress on inflation. The U.S. Federal Reserve (the Fed) cut rates by 25 basis points in November and December. Although, the Fed adopted a more hawkish stance, projecting fewer rate cuts for 2025 than previously anticipated.[1]
  • Credit sectors performed well over the quarter, as investment grade corporate[2] spreads tightened 10bps, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS)[3] spreads tightened 24 bps, and asset-backed securities (ABS)[4] spreads tightened by 20 bps. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.64% to 4.24% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.79% to 4.57%.[1]

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned -2.78%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended December 31, 2024. The Fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index*, returned -3.06% during the same period.
  • Duration positioning contributed to relative performance over the quarter. Being underweight duration versus the benchmark contributed as U.S. Treasury rates moved higher during the period.
  • Security selection within investment grade corporate bonds contributed to relative performance, specifically within the Financials sector. We reduced the portfolio's overall exposure to investment grade corporate bonds, rotating into other sectors we believe offer better relative value.
  • The portfolio's allocation to ABS and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) modestly contributed to relative performance. Within CLOs, we increased the portfolio's exposure, adding to AAA-rated CLOs. Within ABS, we modestly reduced our overall exposure.
  • The portfolio's allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) detracted from relative performance. We increased the portfolio's exposure to MBS as we believe this is an attractive asset class for high quality carry and it may perform well as rate volatility declines.
  • There were no other significant detractors over the period.

Their durations (a measure of interest-rate sensitivity) typically range between 75% and 125% of the three-year average of the effective duration of the Morningstar Core Bond Index.

A Note about Risk: The Fund is subject to the general risks associated with investing in fixed income securities, including market, credit, liquidity, and interest rate risk. The value of an investment in the Fund will change as interest rates fluctuate in response to market movements. When interest rates rise, the prices of debt securities are likely to decline, and when interest rates fall, the prices of debt securities tend to rise. The Fund may invest in high yield, lower-rated debt securities, sometimes called junk bonds and may involve greater risks than higher rated debt securities. These securities carry increased risks of price volatility, illiquidity, and the possibility of default in the timely payment of interest and principal. The Fund may invest in foreign or emerging market securities, which may be adversely affected by economic, political, or regulatory factors and subject to currency volatility and greater liquidity risk. The Fund may invest in derivatives, which are subject to greater liquidity, leverage, and counterparty risk. These factors can affect Fund performance.

The Fund's portfolio is actively managed and is subject to change.

The credit quality of the securities in a portfolio are assigned by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO), such as Standard & Poor's, Moody's, or Fitch, as an indication of an issuer's creditworthiness. Ratings range from 'AAA' (highest) to 'D' (lowest). Bonds rated `BBB' or above are considered investment grade. Credit ratings 'BB' and below are lower-rated securities (junk bonds). High-yielding, non-investment-grade bonds (junk bonds) involve higher risks than investment-grade bonds. Adverse conditions may affect the issuer's ability to pay interest and principal on these securities.

The performance table above is based on Class I shares. Average Annual Total Returns are based on changes in the net asset value and assume reinvestment of all distributions, and do not reflect deduction of any front-end sales charges which are not applicable for Class I shares. Returns for less than one year are not annualized.

ICE Data Indices, LLC ("ICE"), used with permission. ICE PERMITS USE OF THE ICE BofA INDICES AND RELATED DATA ON AN "AS IS" BASIS, MAKES NO WARRANTIES REGARDING SAME, DOES NOT GUARANTEE THE SUITABILITY, QUALITY, ACCURACY, TIMELINESS, AND/OR COMPLETENESS OF THE ICE BofA INDICES OR ANY DATA INCLUDED IN, RELATED TO, OR DERIVED THEREFROM, ASSUMES NO LIABILITY IN CONNECTION WITH THE USE OF THE FOREGOING, AND DOES NOT SPONSOR, ENDORSE, OR RECOMMEND LORD ABBETT, OR ANY OF ITS PRODUCTS OR SERVICES.

An asset-backed security (ABS) is a type of financial investment that is collateralized by an underlying pool of assets-usually ones that generate a cash flow from debt, such as loans, leases, credit card balances, or receivables.

Mortgage-backed securities are variations of asset-backed securities that are formed by pooling together mortgages exclusively.

Commercial mortgage-backed securities are fixed-income investment products that are backed by mortgages on commercial properties rather than residential real estate.

A collateralized loan obligation (CLO) is a single security backed by a pool of debt. Collateralized loan obligations (CLO) are often backed by corporate loans with low credit ratings or loans taken out by private equity firms to conduct leveraged buyouts.

The views and information discussed in this commentary are as of December 31, 2024, are subject to change, and may not reflect the views of the firm as a whole. The views expressed in market commentaries are at a specific point in time, are opinions only, and should not be relied upon as a forecast, research, or investment advice regarding a particular investment or the markets in general. data.title: Trump-Era Tariffs: Impact On Trade And Emerging Market Bonds
data.description:
Transcript

Eric Fine - So what's happened with tariffs under the new Trump administration? Well, tariffs were announced on Canada and Mexico at an expected 25%. But more or less immediately, within 24 hours, got a one-month extension in exchange for some strategic concessions, not on economic issues.

Tariffs were also announced on China at 10%. But the response was number one targeted, and number two didn't include soybeans. So these are indications that they're saving those issues for a compromise later.

And overall, you could argue that the Trump administration's response has been sensitive to the market impact of China. It is a tit-for-tat game, unlike the potentially one-off 'chicken' games with Mexico, Colombia, and Panama. But it seems bounded. And the Chinese response was consistent with that view.

Last but most importantly, the Chinese foreign exchange fix, the CNY fix, is their most important signaling mechanism for months now. It has been stable to better than expected by the survey the morning before the fix. It's a really strong sign of stability and of calm. It could be held up as a threat if negotiations go bad. But if the negotiations between the US and China end up with 10% tariffs and a 10% CNY deval, even Treasury Secretary Bessent is going to say that's a loss.

So we think one of the modalities, probably central case, is an agreement on currency stability with the US, whatever gets agreed on tariffs. And that's important because that's the only thing you can really care about in bonds, is how does this transmit?

If the FX is stable, we expect the same thing we've seen for last five years, which is Asia gets anchored. Remember, Asian interest rates are now the same as US interest rates, broadly speaking. EM Asian is the biggest thing that's happened in last five years, and that will get anchored. So that's what's happened, and the market is trying to figure out what stage are we at, and how are things going to be going forward.

So I told you what happened in terms of the Trump administration's tariffs and the response from China. What does this mean for asset prices in general? Well, I think there are two, maybe three big implications.

The first is interest rates down. Why? It's the biggest short in the market still. And a lot of the concerns are projections, in our opinion, of fiscal concerns on Trump that say more about the speaker than about reality. There may be fiscal concerns about Trump that are legitimate. We think it's way too early, and we think it's more or less a hollow argument.

The other one is potentially dollar down. It's the second-biggest position in the market, particularly against euro, some against CNY, CNH, some still against dollar peso. And that could reverse, especially in the case of Mexico. But that's a second big implication is dollar down.

The third is, and this is a little more speculative, it might be risk on. To the extent that these concerns that the market has had, not just since President Trump's election, but before Trump's election, may be peaking, right? Either peaking or resolved. And that will be the elimination of a big period of uncertainty. And what are you going to be left with if this is gone? What you're going to be left with is a US that's stimulating. Remember, taxes are being cut, regulations are being cut, that's stimulative. And China is at risk of stimulating.

The property sector has been a really interesting sector for us, but the bottom line of it is we think a lot of the major issues in the property sector may be over. What over means for us is we don't find cheap things there anymore. The majors are established.

So this is a pretty neat context. So I summarized what the Trump administration has done on tariffs. I told you what I think it means for asset prices in general, what does it mean for our portfolio of EM bonds in particular.

For months, we have been trying to establish a position that reflects what will happen once these risks are either priced or addressed. And the main way we had done that was having an overweight in local currency. Local currency overall did poorly, however, not if you didn't own Mexico.

So our number one response will continue to be bottom-up finding the countries, not some top-down response. So we're very, very selective. And last year, our best performer was not owning Mexico. And in January, our best performer was owning Mexico. And so we think that's one really important implication is country-specific.

So Mexico is a big part of our answer, but the real answer is country by country. This may have been a game of chicken for Mexico, Colombia and Panama. And the most important feature of a game of chicken is that it's only one round. And that one round may be over. We don't know yet.

Another big part of our answer is going to be long duration. So we have an overweight relative to our benchmark and duration. But real rates in a lot of EMs were especially high, both policy rates and market rates. So you could see this duration rally really be turbocharged in some of the EM local currency bond markets, as well as their long-dated dollar-denominated bonds. So we have some century bonds that have a lot of upsides if rates go down and not a lot of downsides if rates go higher.

So those are our main reactions. We're maintaining our attractiveness to duration and continuing to have exposure to the world as it's going to look when this is priced or resolved.

Important Disclosures
